title: Economic sectors
summary: 9609 AS — primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary sectors and structural change.
learningObjectives: Define primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary sectors.
Give examples of businesses in each sector.
Explain sectoral change in developed economies.
Apply sector classification to case firms.

simpleExplanation.title: Where activity happens
simpleExplanation.summary: Economies divide into primary (raw materials), secondary (manufacturing), tertiary (services), and quaternary (knowledge/R&D). Developed countries shift toward services and knowledge work.

flashcards.back: Wheat farm, oil extraction, commercial fishing.
Car factory, house builder, food processor.
Software R&D, university research, data analytics.
Developed economies: declining primary/secondary share, rising tertiary/quaternary.
Higher incomes demand services; automation reduces manufacturing jobs.
Manufacturing may relocate to lower-cost countries.
Vertical integration spans sectors — e.g. oil firm extracts and refines.

sections.content: **Economic sectors** (1.2.1) — classify **case firms** quickly in exams. Note **structural change** when discussing developed vs developing economies.

sections.question: UK economy: primary 1%, secondary 18%, tertiary 81% of employment. Explain implications for a school leaver choosing a career.
sections.solution:
**Few primary jobs** — agriculture/mining mechanised, limited openings.

**Secondary declining relatively** — some **advanced manufacturing** remains but **automation** (4.1.3) reduces headcount.

**Tertiary dominates** — **retail, health, finance, tourism** offer most vacancies — skills in **customer service, digital, care** valuable.

**Quaternary growing** — **STEM, coding, R&D** for higher-paid roles.

**Business implication:** Firms need **training and HRM** (2.1) for service-quality staff.
